video_title: Open Source Wins, AGI Is Here, and Scorsese’s AI Toolkit with CEOs of Cerebras & Black Forest Labs
gist: Jason Calacanis interviews Cerebras CEO Andrew Feldman and Black Forest Labs CEO Robin Rombach. Feldman details an unprecedented, demand-booked AI data-center buildout, Cerebras' $25B backlog and inference-speed edge, argues AGI is already here and open-source/sovereign models are winning the enterprise long tail; Rombach lays out the path from latent-diffusion image models to multimodal world-action models that double as robot brains, plus his Scorsese collaboration.

insights.claim: Andrew Feldman says AI data centers under construction will, over the next several years, consume more power than the entire Earth used in the previous 50 years, with individual football-field-sized buildings drawing more power than midsize cities.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Frames power generation and grid interconnect as the true bottleneck and the highest-leverage adjacent trade to the AI buildout.
insights.quote: what we're talking about now are data centers that are in the next several years going to use more power than the previous 50 years on Earth took

insights.claim: The AI data-center buildout has gone global into second- and third-tier nations, with Feldman citing construction across the US, Canada, the Nordics, France, the Middle East, plus Kazakhstan, Tajikistan, Georgia and Armenia.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Signals sovereign-compute demand and power availability, not just US hyperscaler capex, is driving siting decisions worldwide.

insights.claim: Cerebras carries a $25 billion backlog, with customers placing chip orders before the chips are even finished being built.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: A concrete backlog figure for a public inference-chip pure-play; a hard demand data point to track against Nvidia and neocloud order books.
insights.quote: we have a $25 billion backlog

insights.claim: Feldman argues this AI cycle is unusual because demand is already booked rather than speculative—builders are 'trying to capture yesterday's demand,' with capacity outstripping the ability to build data centers and fill them with hardware.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Counters the 'if you build it they will come' bubble narrative; if true, near-term overcapacity risk is lower than bears assume.
insights.quote: They're trying to capture yesterday's demand

insights.claim: Feldman frames hyperscalers building their own chips (OpenAI, Amazon) as a move to control their destiny and avoid single-supplier dependency—echoing lessons from x86 hyperscalers' reliance on Intel—not necessarily to build the fastest chip.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Custom silicon is about supply/pricing leverage against Nvidia, not displacing it; supports a multi-vendor accelerator market rather than winner-take-all.
insights.quote: nobody likes being dependent

insights.claim: Feldman claims Cerebras broke the ~18-month doubling of Moore's law with its current chip and expects to deliver well over 2x improvement in the next 18 months, because a new architecture has more optimization headroom than the 20-year-old GPU architecture.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: A faster-than-Moore's-law roadmap is the core bull case for Cerebras taking inference share as GPUs lean on shrinking fab nodes.
insights.quote: my view is in the next 18 months we'll be way over 2x

insights.claim: Feldman argues reasoning is inference and is computationally intensive, so blisteringly fast compute is what makes long internal reasoning chains tractable rather than prohibitively slow.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Positions inference-speed leaders as direct beneficiaries of the shift from single-shot answers to token-heavy reasoning workloads.
insights.quote: this reasoning consumes a huge amount of tokens internally

insights.claim: Feldman says Cerebras is roughly 15x faster, so running a reasoning job for 24 hours on its hardware yields the equivalent of weeks or months of thinking.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Speed compounds into qualitatively more reasoning depth per unit time—the value proposition for premium-priced fast inference.
insights.quote: what what if by using Cerebrus we were 15 times faster and then you ran it for 24 hours

insights.claim: Feldman notes each Cerebras wafer-scale chip costs about half a billion dollars to make, underscoring the capital intensity behind the architecture.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Context on the fixed-cost moat and why backlog visibility matters for the economics.
insights.quote: when one costs half a billion to make you you bring it everywhere with you

insights.claim: Feldman says fast chips reduce the latency cost of safety guardrails, a benefit Cerebras discovered in the prior six weeks as guardrails otherwise add time and make models feel slower.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: A non-obvious commercial angle: speed offsets the UX tax of compliance/guardrails, a selling point as regulation tightens.
insights.quote: one of the things that fast does is it makes the guard rails less painful

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis says the open-source vs. frontier gap has closed sharply this year, recounting that after smart-routing between Claude and Kimmy he couldn't tell the difference once open-source models figured out reasoning.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Erodes pricing power for closed frontier labs on commodity tasks; watch token-price compression and routing tools.
insights.quote: this Kimmy, I can't tell the difference

insights.claim: Feldman's 'Ferrari vs. minivan' framework holds that hard frontier problems go to OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini while a large long tail of ordinary enterprise work only needs rock-solid open-source models.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Implies most enterprise token volume migrates to cheaper open models—bullish for inference cost-per-token providers, bearish for frontier margins on routine work.
insights.quote: you you don't want to take your your Ferrari to the grocery store

insights.claim: Feldman warns the US lacks domestic open-source options—enterprises today choose between OpenAI's OSS model or Chinese models—and argues America needs more domestic open-source models to give the world a choice.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: A geopolitical gap and opportunity; whoever ships credible US open-source models captures sovereignty-conscious buyers now defaulting to Chinese weights.
insights.quote: If they want to run open source right now, it's OSS 12B or Chinese models

insights.claim: Feldman says regulated industries (finance, healthcare, HIPAA, FINRA) are choosing on-prem open-source deployments over frontier models due to data-leakage and 'sovereignty of intelligence' concerns, and Cerebras is seeing this demand now.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Confirms a real enterprise pull toward controllable, on-prem open models—a tailwind for inference hardware and open-weight ecosystems.

insights.claim: Feldman says Cerebras runs a wide model portfolio—GLM, Kimmy, Qwen, OpenAI's closed models, plus custom models for GSK and UAE partners G42/MBZUAI—positioning it as a neutral, model-agnostic inference layer.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Neutrality across Chinese, US and sovereign models is a strategic moat as customers demand optionality.

insights.claim: Jason notes Nvidia is reluctant to promote its own open-source models because doing so would put it in competition with major customers like Sam Altman, Dario Amodei, Elon Musk and Sergey Brin.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Channel conflict constrains Nvidia's model ambitions, leaving room for neutral players like Cerebras to champion open weights.

insights.claim: Feldman asserts AGI has already been achieved—just not fully deployed—because current reasoning systems blow past the Turing test and any definition of general intelligence set 10-50 years ago.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: A prominent chip CEO declaring AGI is here reframes the debate toward deployment/economics rather than capability milestones.
insights.quote: by any definition we had 20 years ago we've hit it

insights.claim: The biggest capability leap of the past 24 months, per the discussion, is models moving from great summarizers/researchers to understanding user intent and abstracting the solution—eliminating the need to be a 'prompt whisperer.'
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Intent-understanding lowers the skill barrier and expands the addressable user base, accelerating enterprise adoption.
insights.quote: we would never have been able to predict in a short 24 months that it would go from being a great summarizer researcher of web results

insights.claim: Feldman argues recursive/'loop-maxing' gains are exponential and no one knows where they stop—each loop yields vastly better, not marginally better, answers as you keep throwing compute at the problem.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: If loops scale super-linearly with compute, demand for inference is effectively unbounded—core to the insatiable-demand thesis.
insights.quote: powerful recursive gains are are exponential

insights.claim: Jason equates unlimited tokens with unlimited reasoning, describing a Bittensor project giving him uncapped GLM capacity (likely from cheap Chinese energy) to run hourly autonomous trend-hunting jobs where he watched a reasoning model debate itself over sources.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Illustrates emerging 'always-on' agentic workflows and how cheap distributed compute (crypto networks, Chinese energy) could undercut token pricing.
insights.quote: It opened my eyes just this morning of what a world of unlimited tokens might look like

insights.claim: Feldman uses a genetics analogy that AI compresses the intergenerational learning gap—like studying fruit flies over thousands of generations versus human 15-20 year cycles—because paradigms only shift when people die, and AI removes that delay.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: A mental model for why AI-driven R&D could compound far faster than historical technology diffusion.
insights.quote: we're getting sort of learning so quickly over the equivalent of thousands of generations

insights.claim: The discussion references the US government asking a lab to roll out a powerful new model in stages over cyber-warfare concerns; Feldman calls staged rollout and government red-teaming reasonable, analogizing to how powerful pharmaceuticals are gated.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Signals a normalizing of pre-release government review for frontier models—a regulatory overhang labs and investors must price in.
insights.quote: let's at least do some red teaming at the government so we know our defenses can block this

insights.claim: Jason relays that Palo Alto Networks' Nikesh Arora tested a new model against their own software, it found critical unknown vulnerabilities in an hour, and the team had to stop everything to patch for six weeks.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence of AI's offensive cyber capability crossing a threshold—bullish for security spend, and a real dual-use risk.
insights.quote: we had to stop everything we're doing and do patches for 6 weeks

insights.claim: Feldman warns a massive data breach or 'black swan' AI security event is effectively certain, invoking Warren Buffett's reinsurance framing that you must reserve for a disaster whose timing is unknown.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Reinforces structural demand for cyber-insurance and security infrastructure as AI capabilities scale.
insights.quote: there will be a massive breach

insights.claim: Feldman argues political polarization actively degrades clear thinking on AI policy and that the AI industry should do a better job regulating itself before government does it, noting rank-and-file government workers are trying hard but inventing the playbook as they go.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Sets expectations for messy, reactive AI policy—self-regulation vs. legislation is the key fork to watch.
insights.quote: I I think the polarization hurts a great deal. It hurts clear thinking

insights.claim: Feldman's headline optimistic thesis is that AI gives a real shot at ensuring no one in the next generations dies of cancer, framing this as the pro side of a ledger that outweighs economic dislocation.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: The abundance narrative underpinning capex justification—biotech/AI-for-science is the payoff investors are underwriting.
insights.quote: we have a shot with this technology. So not our children nor anyone they know dies of cancer

insights.claim: Feldman contends personalized AI tutors could finally deliver Aristotle-to-Alexander one-on-one teaching that we've known is superior for a thousand years but never scaled beyond 'factory farming' to a mid-level.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: Points to education as a large near-term AI application where value is high and current delivery is poor.
insights.quote: Imagine if we built agents that that taught children for their way of learning

insights.claim: Feldman acknowledges real economic dislocation ahead—analogizing to carriage- and horse-related jobs displaced by cars—but insists the abundance ledger (unlimited energy, calories, knowledge, education, housing) is heavily weighted positive.
insights.speaker: Andrew Feldman
insights.so_what: A balanced-but-bullish framing of AI labor impact useful for anticipating policy and sentiment swings.
insights.quote: the ledger, as you describe it, is heavily weighted towards abundance

themes.theme: Generative Media & World-Action Models (Black Forest Labs)

insights.claim: Robin Rombach says his team invented latent diffusion—the foundational algorithm behind essentially all generative image, video and physical-AI models—which compresses natural data (like JPEG/MP3 do) then trains a transformer on that efficient representation.
insights.speaker: Robin Rombach
insights.so_what: Establishes Black Forest Labs' foundational IP credibility across the entire generative-media stack.
insights.quote: latent diffusion which is basically like the fundamental algorithm behind all of like generative models

insights.claim: Rombach says Black Forest Labs is entering a new paradigm combining multimodal visual models (image/video/audio) with action prediction, so the same model can generate media and be deployed as a brain on a real-world robot.
insights.speaker: Robin Rombach
insights.so_what: Convergence of generative media and robotics means image/video labs are also robotics-foundation-model players—expanding their TAM.
insights.quote: you can actually use the same model to make images to make videos to make audio and to predict actions

insights.claim: Rombach argues pre-training on video yields implicit understanding of real-world physics and object interactions, which is what enables action prediction and robotics to fall out of the same model.
insights.speaker: Robin Rombach
insights.so_what: The technical rationale for why video-generation leaders have a path into embodied AI.
insights.quote: pre-training on videos gives like implicit understanding of the physics of interactions with the real world

insights.claim: Rombach frames intelligence as two complementary layers—intuitive (approached via images, less compute-intensive) and deep reasoning—that must interact for a complete system.
insights.speaker: Robin Rombach
insights.so_what: Suggests image/vision models and reasoning LLMs are converging rather than competing.
insights.quote: there's like intuitive intelligence and then there's like a deep reasoning layer

insights.claim: Rombach is skeptical that fully generating movies from a video model is the ultimate goal, arguing the highest-value use is a human-in-the-loop medium for iteration, where the most interesting outputs actually emerge.
insights.speaker: Robin Rombach
insights.so_what: Tempers the 'AI replaces filmmakers' hype; positions tools as creative augmentation, shaping how media companies should invest.
insights.quote: I'm not sure if that is like the ultimate goal

insights.claim: Rombach describes collaborating in-person with Martin Scorsese, who used the models to pull a mental picture of a scene (a village in Eastern Europe) out of his head and iterate on visual outputs—storyboarding/pre-visualization rather than final footage.
insights.speaker: Robin Rombach
insights.so_what: A marquee validation of generative tools in elite filmmaking, but scoped to ideation—clarifies the realistic near-term use case.
insights.quote: getting like the mental picture of something out of your head and communicating it in a visual way

insights.claim: Jason relays that Gal Gadot's ~$30M-budget Bitcoin movie was shot on a sound stage with no green screens and GenAI-generated scenery, which she said would have cost $150M with physical sets and never been green-lit otherwise.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A concrete cost-collapse data point (5x) showing GenAI already changing which films get financed—disruptive to VFX/set economics.
insights.quote: She said it would have cost $150 million if they had to build sets and the film would have never been green lit

insights.claim: Rombach says Black Forest Labs blocks generation of protected IP on its public tools while separately building custom models jointly with IP holders, some on open-source and some on more powerful proprietary models.
insights.speaker: Robin Rombach
insights.so_what: A workable commercial model for content libraries; the dual open/proprietary approach is how media IP gets monetized safely.

insights.claim: Jason argues the future for IP holders is licensing AI creation to fans—citing 'Star Wars Stories Untold' fan films already getting millions of views per video—letting the customer base pay to create authorized derivative content.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A new revenue model where fans generate content under license—relevant to how media companies deploy GenAI on platforms like Disney+.

insights.claim: Rombach says Black Forest Labs, based in Freiburg (Black Forest, Germany) and San Francisco, recently raised a large round, crossed 100 employees, and is hiring researchers in diffusion/flow-matching training and engineers for physical-AI customer deployments and large-scale compute infra.
insights.speaker: Robin Rombach
insights.so_what: Company scale/funding signal for a leading open-source (Flux) generative-media startup and its expansion into physical AI.
insights.quote: we just crossed 100 people

insights.claim: Rombach says image/video editing has evolved from text-to-image, to text+image-to-image (iterating on a real or generated image), to combining multiple images plus text into new content—and the same principle now applies to video.
insights.speaker: Robin Rombach
insights.so_what: Maps the capability progression that turns generative tools from a 'slot machine' into controllable production instruments.

insights.claim: Rombach says robots today run diverse hardware with different action representations, so current practice pre-trains broad visual understanding then uses only a few hours of fine-tuning data per task—the goal being to shift toward as much in-context prompting of robots as possible.
insights.speaker: Robin Rombach
insights.so_what: Defines the current bottleneck and roadmap for embodied AI—moving from per-robot fine-tuning to language-model-style in-context control.
insights.quote: you have like all this like visual understanding in the models um and then you need only a very little bit of like a few hours of um fine-tuning data
